item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Barbary lion bit bothered me. Barbary lions are extinct. It's true there's a few zoos claiming they have Barbary lions, but trust me, they're full of shit. At best, they have hybrids: sub-Saharan African lions with some Barbary (which are from the northern part of Africa) in their bloodline. The Rabat Zoo in Morocco had the strongest claim to modern Barbary lions, with theirs supposedly having been descended from the king's private collection of Barbary lions. Genetic testing to determine if they were pure Barbary lions was done on 5 of their 35. None of them passed the test.

This doesn't surprise me much. Even with still living animals, zoos are far less accurate than you'd expect them to be with species which are difficult to distinguish visually. Recently 16 samples from zoos claiming their crocodiles were Crocodylus Niloticus (Nile crocodiles) showed that only ONE was Crocodylus Niloticus. The rest were Crocodylus Suchus (West African crocodile), a much smaller and less aggressive species. But I digress. Barbary lions are gone. I doubt the best scientists in the world would be fooled into thinking otherwise by a shady zookeeper.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get why they don't want to test it - they don't know if it foretells or creates the future. You don't know what powers you're messing with.

What I'm having a hard time believing is that they have that much trouble finding personnel that would rather NOT know that their days are numbered. There's really that many people who are that eager to know when they're going to die? Personally, I'd rather not have that knowledge - I'd prefer to keep living like I'm going to live forever, and be ambushed by it. Having an endpoint would just hang over me like a dark cloud. I can't be the only one that thinks this way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No-vote for me.

Obviously the author put a lot of work and thought into this article, but there's a bit of lolFoundation stuff in here that I'm not a fan of (the idea that the termination testing didn't stop after they killed seven of their own personnel kind of boggles the mind), and I could have done without the "what does the clock consider to be human" line (just seemed in poor taste, didn't add anything to the story for me). I get that this is partly why most of the article is set 50+ years in the past, but it doesn't work for me, personally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually disliked it, though I dig part of the premise.

First, it is clearly Safe. The impulse of people to know their fate is completely mundane and do not come from the SCP in any meaningful way. It actually never broke containment, people broke protocol, and even this in a reasonably minor way for the Foundation.

Second, the effort to keep it away from people hugely outweigh the benefit. Seriously, the Foundation can't even say for sure that this thing creates the outcome, not just predicts it. Some of the predictions put this death far in the future.

Given the kind of thing those people work with, if one can't deal with her own mortality, she isn't cut for the Foundation anyway.

I like the idea of working the psychology of people dealing with this kind of knowledge, and this part of the article is well delivered, as much as the experiments done. But the rest of the article is too much afraid of too little danger. And most of the experiments denied could have been done, by the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the direction you took with this, given how this would actually affect Foundation personnel in a not unwholly bad way, and thus make it a challenge for them to contain. The hints you give about a greater force being the one behind such an object (and also having its eye, whatever it is, on the Foundation) are enough for me to wonder in a positive way. I dig this quite a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I thought there was way too much addended material, but damn if it doesn’t come together. Part of me still feels like the utility of this to the Foundation would outweigh the risks. A sharper, saner mind than Braun’s could divise case by case expsure logistics to assess the viability of various experiments or missions (for example, if a team would all expected to be killed in a given experiment, hey, we’ve got a magic actuaty for that). I’m not saying the scp wouldn’t prove too unpredictable, but the Foundation usually learns those lessons the hard way.

On the other hand, this is brilliantly put together already, making the most of a simple idea, so maybe ‘leave the damn thing alone’ is good policy for both the object and the scp. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one, The idea of them having to make all these extra security measures to keep their own people from screwing with this thing is really interesting.
+1 from me,

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I definitely wanted to hit that note, but I also wanted it to be ambiguous:

1. Was it really God or some other malevolent being wanting to use Ignatius for its purposes?

After all, Ignatius is a monk, so appearing to him as a version of an angry Judeo-Christian God is a logical approach to get him to do something.

2. Is it there to punish the Foundation? Future generations or humanity as a whole? Specific people who won't be able to stop themselves from using it? Something else?

Mostly I wanted to to try to get on paper the feeling — instilled in me through years of quasi-religious education — that all my problems in life, all my sense of unease, stems from some omnipotent quasi-omnipotent entity constantly raging because I'm not kissing its ass 24/7.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kind of get the implication this was made on God's orders specifically to punish the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just rewrote and expanded "Breach 1955-F" — on reflection, the psychology of people using this thing without authorization needed some more exposition.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! Just did this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, I just hosted the image locally — hopefully it will work for you now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there a way to host the image here? I didn't realize that.

Yes. Ideally, one would always localize an image used on an SCP article. The How to Write an SCP Guide, "Formatting" tab has the directions. Look at the "Placing an Image or Media File" section.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, I put a copy of the image on imgur. That must be it.

Is there a way to host the image here? I didn't realize that. I'm at work now, but if so, I'll figure this out and fix it tonight when I come home.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's working for me.

Is it hosted on a third party website? If so, various blocking software used at schools/jobs may block the image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmm….I tested in both Chrome and IE, and it seemed to work ok. Could someone else confirm whether the image is/is not working for them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image isn't working for me, not sure why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Which classification would you suggest? Do you think Euclid would work better?

Edit: I thought some more about it, and Euclid is probably better here (which is likely what you were thinking), so I changed it. When I originally made it Keter, I focused on (i) it keeps breaking containment (ii) the possibility that this thing will cause an XK. However, arguably it hasn't caused an XK by now so….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
when he was mauled and subsequently consumed by a pack of bears.

The fuck, clock?

I'm not quite sure how I feel about this one yet. I know that there's precedent for fudging the classifications of objects to keep people doing what they're supposed to, and I certainly understand the temptation to look at the clock (I'd like to, too!), but something's not working for me here. I'll come back to it later and re-decide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, it's keter because too many people have used it despite its obvious danger? I can't say I saw that coming. Upvoted. Although, why was an Overseer allowed access to it? They're A class, no access to anomalies. Why one that has reliably killed everyone exposed to its effects?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First, the image is licensed under creative commons: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hampton_Court_astronomical_clock#/media/File:Hampton_Court_Astrological_Clock.jpg

Second, thanks to Adrian Godet for giving me feedback in the drafts forum. Thanks also to various people on /r/rational who had thoughts on how such an item might be used, especially omegaCurator who suggested the bit about Dr. Bright.

Finally, to forestall possible accusations of plagiarism, I should disclose that the speech in the vision of St. Ignatius is a (lightly edited) collage of verses from the King James Version.
